records.ety: [F. exprs, L. expressus, p. p. of exprimere to express; ex. out + premere To press. See Press.]

quotes: Their human countenance The express resemblance of the gods.

defs: Directly and distinctly stated; declared in terms; not implied or left to inference; made unambiguous by intention and care; clear; not dubious; as, express consent; an express statement.
Intended for a particular purpose; relating to an express; sent on a particular errand; dispatched with special speed; as, an express messenger or train. Also used adverbially.
quotes: A messenger sent express from the other world.

defs: of or pertaining to an express train or other conveyance designated an express{5}; making few or no intermediate stops; as, an express stop; an express fare; an express elevator.
A clear image or representation; an expression; a plain declaration.
quotes: The only remanent express of Christ's sacrifice on earth.

defs: A messenger sent on a special errand; a courier; hence, a regular and fast conveyance; commonly, a company or system for the prompt and safe transportation of merchandise or parcels.

quotes: She charged him . . . to ask at the express if anything came up from town.

defs: That which is sent by an express messenger or message.

a railway train or bus for transporting passengers or goods with speed and punctuality; a train or bus that does not stop at certain stations. Contrasted to local; as, take the express to get there faster.

records.ety: [Cf. OF. espresser, expresser, L. exprimere, expressum. See Express, a.; cf. Sprain.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Expressed(?); p. pr. & vb. n. Expressing.]

defs: To press or squeeze out; as, to express the juice of grapes, or of apples; hence, to extort; to elicit.
quotes: All the fruits out of which drink is expressed.
And th'idle breath all utterly expressed.
Halters and racks can not express from thee More than by deeds.

defs: To make or offer a representation of; to show by a copy or likeness; to represent; to resemble.
quotes: Each skillful artist shall express thy form.
So kids and whelps their sires and dams express.

defs: To give a true impression of; to represent and make known; to manifest plainly; to show in general; to exhibit, as an opinion or feeling, by a look, gesture, and esp. by language; to declare; to utter; to tell.
quotes: They expressed in their lives those excellent doctrines of morality.

defs: To make known the opinions or feelings of; to declare what is in the mind of; to show (one's self); to cause to appear; -- used reflexively.
quotes: Mr. Phillips did express with much indignation against me, one evening.
Moses and Aaron took these men, which are expressed by their names.

defs: To send by express messenger; to forward by special opportunity, or through the medium of an express; as, to express a package.
to produce products that cause the appearance of the corresponding phenotype; -- of a gene or of an organism with a specific gene; as, to express the beta-galactosidase gene,
